(B) 23$$ minutes
(C) 39$$ minutes
(D) 47$$ minutes
(E) 73$$ minutes

(A) 86
(B) 88
(C) 90
(D) 94
content.problem_plain: Manjari s average score on six tests is 82 . Her average score on the 7^ th and 8^ th tests is 98 . What is her average score on all eight tests? ． (A) 86 (B) 88 (C) 90 (D) 94
content.solution_latex: Total score on the first six tests is $$6\times82=492$$. Her total score on all eight tests is $$492+2\times98=688$$, and her average score is $$688\div8=86$$.
content.solution_plain: Total score on the first six tests is 6 82=492 . Her total score on all eight tests is 492+2 98=688 , and her average score is 688 8=86 .

Her total score on all eight tests is $$492+2\times98=688$$, and her average score is $$688\div8=86$$.

If your average score on your first six mathematics tests was $84$ and your average score on your first seven mathematics tests was $85$, then your score on the seventh test was ．
(A) 86
(B) 88
(C) 90
(D) 91
(E) 92
content.problem_plain: If your average score on your first six mathematics tests was 84 and your average score on your first seven mathematics tests was 85 , then your score on the seventh test was ． (A) 86 (B) 88 (C) 90 (D) 91 (E) 92
content.solution_latex: The total score of the first six tests was $84\times6=504$, and the total score of the first seven tests was $85\times7=595$. Therefore, the score of the seventh test equals to the difference: $595-504=91$.

content.problem_latex:
My mom\textquotesingle s birthday is on Sunday, and my dad\textquotesingle s birthday is $$55$$ days later. On what day of the week will my dad\textquotesingle s birthday be?
(A) Sunday
(B) Monday
(C) Tuesday
(D) Thursday
(E) Saturday
content.problem_plain: My mom s birthday is on Sunday, and my dad s birthday is 55 days later. On what day of the week will my dad s birthday be? (A) Sunday (B) Monday (C) Tuesday (D) Thursday (E) Saturday
content.solution_latex: After $$1$$ day, it is Monday; after $$2$$ days, it is Tuesday; after $$3$$ days, it is Wednesday $$\cdots$$ After $$8$$ days, it is still Monday, and the period is $$7$$ days. $$55\div 7=7$$(weeks) $$\rm R$$ $$6$$ (days). The birthday is Saturday.
content.solution_plain: After 1 day, it is Monday; after 2 days, it is Tuesday; after 3 days, it is Wednesday After 8 days, it is still Monday, and the period is 7 days. 55 7=7 (weeks) R 6 (days). The birthday is Saturday.

step_outline: After $$1$$ day, it is Monday; after $$2$$ days, it is Tuesday; after $$3$$ days, it is Wednesday $$\cdots$$ After $$8$$ days, it is still Monday, and the period is $$7$$ days.
The birthday is Saturday.

content.problem_latex:
On a map of England and Wales the distance between St Ives in Cornwall and St Ives in Cambridgeshire measures $$18\text{cm}$$. In reality the distance between the two towns is about $$450\text{km}$$.  Which of the options below is the scale of the map?
(A) 1:2500000
(B) 1:1000000
(C) 1:750000
(D) 1:500000
(E) 1:250000
content.problem_plain: On a map of England and Wales the distance between St Ives in Cornwall and St Ives in Cambridgeshire measures 18 cm . In reality the distance between the two towns is about 450 km . Which of the options below is the scale of the map? (A) 1:2500000 (B) 1:1000000 (C) 1:750000 (D) 1:500000 (E) 1:250000
content.solution_latex: The scale of the map is $$18\text{cm} : 450\text{km}$$; converting the actual distance to centimetres, this is equivalent to $$18 : 450 \times 1000 \times 100 = 18 : 45 000 000$$. Simplifying gives a scale of $$1 : 2 500 000$$.
content.solution_plain: The scale of the map is 18 cm : 450 km ; converting the actual distance to centimetres, this is equivalent to 18 : 450 1000 100 = 18 : 45 000 000 . Simplifying gives a scale of 1 : 2 500 000 .

step_outline: The scale of the map is $$18\text{cm} : 450\text{km}$$; converting the actual distance to centimetres, this is equivalent to $$18 : 450 \times 1000 \times 100 = 18 : 45 000 000$$.
Simplifying gives a scale of $$1 : 2 500 000$$.

content.problem_latex:
To defeat a dragon, Matthias has to cut off all the dragon\textquotesingle s heads. If he can cut off $$3$$ of the dragon\textquotesingle s heads, one new head immediately grows. Matthias defeats the dragon by cutting off $$17$$ heads in total. How many heads did the dragon have at the beginning?
(A) 8
(B) 9
(C) 10
(D) 11
(E) 12
content.problem_plain: To defeat a dragon, Matthias has to cut off all the dragon s heads. If he can cut off 3 of the dragon s heads, one new head immediately grows. Matthias defeats the dragon by cutting off 17 heads in total. How many heads did the dragon have at the beginning? (A) 8 (B) 9 (C) 10 (D) 11 (E) 12
content.solution_latex: $17\div3=5R2$, so Matthias has $5$ groups of $3$ cuts, which means $5$ new heads grew. Thus, there are $17-5=12$ heads originally.
content.solution_plain: 17 3=5R2 , so Matthias has 5 groups of 3 cuts, which means 5 new heads grew. Thus, there are 17-5=12 heads originally.

step_outline: $17\div3=5R2$, so Matthias has $5$ groups of $3$ cuts, which means $5$ new heads grew.
Thus, there are $17-5=12$ heads originally.

content.problem_latex:
The sales tax rate in Bergville is $$6\textbackslash\%$$. During a sale at the Bergville Coat Closet, the price of a coat is discounted $$20\textbackslash\%$$ from its ＄$$90.00$$ price. Two clerks, Jack and Jill, calculate the bill independently. Jack rings up ＄$$90.00$$ and adds $$6\textbackslash\%$$ sales tax, then subtracts $$20\textbackslash\%$$ from this total. Jill rings up ＄$$90.00$$, subtracts $$20\textbackslash\%$$ of the price, then adds $$6\textbackslash\%$$ of the discounted price for sales tax. What is Jack's total minus Jill's total?
(A) ＄$$-1.06
(B) ＄$$-0.53
(C) ＄$$0
(D) ＄$$0.53
(E) ＄$$1.06
content.problem_plain: The sales tax rate in Bergville is 6 % . During a sale at the Bergville Coat Closet, the price of a coat is discounted 20 % from its ＄ 90.00 price. Two clerks, Jack and Jill, calculate the bill independently. Jack rings up ＄ 90.00 and adds 6 % sales tax, then subtracts 20 % from this total. Jill rings up ＄ 90.00 , subtracts 20 % of the price, then adds 6 % of the discounted price for sales tax. What is Jack's total minus Jill's total? (A) ＄ -1.06 (B) ＄ -0.53 (C) ＄ 0 (D) ＄ 0.53 (E) ＄ 1.06
content.solution_latex: The price Jacks rings up is $$(90.00)(1.06)(0.80)$$. The price Jill rings up is $$(90.00)(0.80)(1.06)$$. By the commutative property of multiplication, these quantities are the same, and the difference is $$(\text{C})$$＄$$0$$.

content.problem_latex:
$$37$$ sakura trees were planted along one side of the road. The trees were planted at $$4m$$ intervals. After drivers complained the road was too pink, pineapple trees were planted on the other side of the road at $$6m$$ intervals. If there were sakura and pineapple trees at both ends of the road, how many pineapple trees were planted? ．
(A) 24
(B) 25
(C) 144
(D) 18
content.problem_plain: 37 sakura trees were planted along one side of the road. The trees were planted at 4m intervals. After drivers complained the road was too pink, pineapple trees were planted on the other side of the road at 6m intervals. If there were sakura and pineapple trees at both ends of the road, how many pineapple trees were planted? ． (A) 24 (B) 25 (C) 144 (D) 18

content.problem_latex:
Alicia, Bryant, and Charles work for a software company and they want to write a particular computer program. Alicia and Bryant first write $\frac{1}{3}$ of the program in $6$ days. Then Bryant and Charles write $\frac{1}{4}$ of the remaining program in $2$ days. In the end three of them work together to finish the program in $5$ days. How many days will it take if Alicia works on the program by herself?
(A) 20
(B) 36
(C) 45
(D) 60
(E) 75
content.problem_plain: Alicia, Bryant, and Charles work for a software company and they want to write a particular computer program. Alicia and Bryant first write 1 3 of the program in 6 days. Then Bryant and Charles write 1 4 of the remaining program in 2 days. In the end three of them work together to finish the program in 5 days. How many days will it take if Alicia works on the program by herself? (A) 20 (B) 36 (C) 45 (D) 60 (E) 75
content.solution_latex: In the second part, Bryant and Charles can write $\left( 1-\frac{1}{3} \right) \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{6}$ of the program in 2 days. Therefore, they can write $\frac{1}{6} \div 2 =\frac{1}{12}$ in one day. There is still $1-\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{6}=\frac{1}{2}$ left.  Now in the last part, they work together and finish in $5$ days. They can write $\frac{1}{2} \div 5=\frac{1}{10}$ in one day. Therefore, Alicia can write $\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{12}=\frac{1}{60}$ in one day, which means she needs $60$ days to complete.
content.solution_plain: In the second part, Bryant and Charles can write ( 1- 1 3 ) 1 4 = 1 6 of the program in 2 days. Therefore, they can write 1 6 2 = 1 12 in one day. There is still 1- 1 3 - 1 6 = 1 2 left. Now in the last part, they work together and finish in 5 days. They can write 1 2 5= 1 10 in one day. Therefore, Alicia can write 1 10 - 1 12 = 1 60 in one day, which means she needs 60 days to complete.

step_outline: In the second part, Bryant and Charles can write $\left( 1-\frac{1}{3} \right) \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{6}$ of the program in 2 days.
Therefore, they can write $\frac{1}{6} \div 2 =\frac{1}{12}$ in one day.
There is still $1-\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{6}=\frac{1}{2}$ left.
Now in the last part, they work together and finish in $5$ days.
They can write $\frac{1}{2} \div 5=\frac{1}{10}$ in one day.
Therefore, Alicia can write $\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{12}=\frac{1}{60}$ in one day, which means she needs $60$ days to complete.

Water from the first faucet fills the swimming pool in $36$ hours. Water from each of the two other faucets fills the same swimming pool $4$ times faster. In how many hours will the swimming pool be filled if all three faucets are opened?
(A) 2
(B) 3
(C) 4
(D) 5
content.problem_plain: Water from the first faucet fills the swimming pool in 36 hours. Water from each of the two other faucets fills the same swimming pool 4 times faster. In how many hours will the swimming pool be filled if all three faucets are opened? (A) 2 (B) 3 (C) 4 (D) 5
content.solution_latex: The efficiency of the first faucet is $\frac1{36}$ and that of the other two is $\frac4{36}$.  Thus it takes $1\div (\frac1{36}+\frac4{36}\times2)=4$ hours to fill the pool.
content.solution_plain: The efficiency of the first faucet is 1 36 and that of the other two is 4 36 . Thus it takes 1 ( 1 36 + 4 36 2)=4 hours to fill the pool.

content.problem_latex:
Some students are going to an amusement park. If each student carries $9$ bottles of water in his or her backpack, there will be $2$ students left carrying nothing; if one of the students carries $2$ bottles of water and the rest students carry $8$ bottles each, all the bottles of water can be carried to the park. There are~\uline{~~~~~~~~~~}~bottles of water in total that need to be carried.
(A) 80
(B) 90
(C) 92
(D) 102
content.problem_plain: Some students are going to an amusement park. If each student carries 9 bottles of water in his or her backpack, there will be 2 students left carrying nothing; if one of the students carries 2 bottles of water and the rest students carry 8 bottles each, all the bottles of water can be carried to the park. There are~ ~~~~~~~~~~ ~bottles of water in total that need to be carried. (A) 80 (B) 90 (C) 92 (D) 102
content.solution_latex: Solve the problem as a problem of surplus and shortage. If each student carries $9$ bottles, there will be a shortage of $9\times2=18$ bottles;  if each student carries $8$ bottles, there will be a shortage of $8-2=6$ bottles. Therefore, there are $(18-6)\div(9-8)=12$ students and $12\times9-18=90$ bottles of water.
content.solution_plain: Solve the problem as a problem of surplus and shortage. If each student carries 9 bottles, there will be a shortage of 9 2=18 bottles; if each student carries 8 bottles, there will be a shortage of 8-2=6 bottles. Therefore, there are (18-6) (9-8)=12 students and 12 9-18=90 bottles of water.

Therefore, there are $(18-6)\div(9-8)=12$ students and $12\times9-18=90$ bottles of water.

content.problem_plain: Ala, Lenka and Miso went out for dessert. Lenka paid 4 dollars and 50 cents for three scoops of ice cream. Miso paid 3 dollars and 60 cents for two cookies. How much did Ala pay for one scoop of ice cream and one cookie? (2011 Math Kangaroo Problem, Level 3 - 4, Question #6) (A) 3 ~dollars and~ 30 ~cents (B) 4 ~dollars and~ 80 ~cents (C) 5 ~dollars and~ 10 ~cents (D) 6 ~dollars and~ 30 ~cents (E) 8 ~dollars and~ 10 ~cents
content.solution_latex: $4$ dollars and $50$ cents are equal to $450$ cents, so one scoop of ice cream costs $450 \div 3 = 150$ cents. $$3$$ dollars and $$60$$ cents are equal to $360$ cents, so one cookie costs $360 \div 2 = 180$ cents. Thus, Ala should pay $ 150 + 180 = 330$ cents for one scoop of ice cream and one cookie. $330$ cents are equal to $3$ dollars and $30$ cents, so the answer is $A$.

content.problem_plain: Nicole has a 500 ml bottle of mouthwash. Every morning, she uses two capfuls of mouthwash. Each capful contains 4 ml of mouthwash. To make coleslaw Cathy uses twice as much carrot (by weight) as cabbage. She then adds half as much yoghurt as cabbage. A pot of Cathy\textquotesingle s coleslaw weighs $$175\text{g}$$. How many pots of coleslaw can she make with a $$2 \text{kg}$$ cabbage?

(B) 40
(C) 50
(D) 60
(E) 80
content.problem_plain: To make coleslaw Cathy uses twice as much carrot (by weight) as cabbage. She then adds half as much yoghurt as cabbage. A pot of Cathy s coleslaw weighs 175 g . How many pots of coleslaw can she make with a 2 kg cabbage? (A) 30 (B) 40 (C) 50 (D) 60 (E) 80
content.solution_latex: The ratio by weight of carrot: cabbage:yoghurt$$=2:1:0.5$$ which we can double to give $$4:2:1$$. We can see that here $$2$$ represents the amount of cabbage and we want $$2 \text{kg}$$ of cabbage, so there will be $$4+2+1=7\text{kg}$$ of coleslaw altogether. Therefore the number of pots is $$7000\div 175 =40$$.

content.problem_plain: Cagney can frost a cupcake every 30 seconds and Lacey can frost a cupcake every 60 seconds. Working together, how many cupcakes can they frost in 5 minutes? (Adapted from 2012 AMC 10 A Problem, Question # 11 ) (A) 10 (B) 15 (C) 20 (D) 25 (E) 30

content.problem_latex:
Mother\textquotesingle s Day in $$2020$$ was May $$10^{\rm th}$$, which was a Sunday. Father\textquotesingle s Day in $$2020$$ was June $$21^{\rm st}$$. On what day of the week did Father\textquotesingle s Day fall?
(A) Monday
(B) Tuesday
(C) Wednesday
(D) Sunday
content.problem_plain: Mother s Day in 2020 was May 10^ th , which was a Sunday. Father s Day in 2020 was June 21^ st . On what day of the week did Father s Day fall? (A) Monday (B) Tuesday (C) Wednesday (D) Sunday
content.solution_latex: There are in total $$21 + 21 = 42$$ days from May $$11$$ to June $$21$$. Since $$42 \div 7 = 6$$, with no remainder, the Father\textquotesingle s Day falls on Sunday as well.
content.solution_plain: There are in total 21 + 21 = 42 days from May 11 to June 21 . Since 42 7 = 6 , with no remainder, the Father s Day falls on Sunday as well.

content.problem_latex:
Some zoo animals are standing in an array to prepare for the Zoo Olympics. The fox, Bob, finds that no matter whether he counts from front to back, back to front, left to right, or right to left, he is always the $$3$$\textsuperscript{rd} in line. How many animals are there in the array?~\uline{~~~~~~~~~~}~
(A) 12
(B) 20
(C) 25
(D) 36
content.problem_plain: Some zoo animals are standing in an array to prepare for the Zoo Olympics. The fox, Bob, finds that no matter whether he counts from front to back, back to front, left to right, or right to left, he is always the 3 rd in line. How many animals are there in the array?~ ~~~~~~~~~~ ~ (A) 12 (B) 20 (C) 25 (D) 36
content.solution_latex: $$3+3-1=5$$.  Since each row and column has $$5$$ animals, there are $$5\times5=25$$ animals in total.
content.solution_plain: 3+3-1=5 . Since each row and column has 5 animals, there are 5 5=25 animals in total.

step_outline: Since each row and column has $$5$$ animals, there are $$5\times5=25$$ animals in total.

content.problem_plain: Anna, Bridgit and Carol run in a 100 m race. When Anna finishes, Bridgit is 16 m behind her and when Bridgit finishes, Carol is 25 m behind her. The girls run at constant speeds throughout the race. How far behind was Carol when Anna finished? (A) 37 m (B) 41 m (C) 50 m (D) 55 m (E) 60 m
content.solution_latex: Carol finishes $$25$$ metres behind Bridgit, so she travels $$75$$ metres while Bridgit runs $$100$$ metres. Therefore she runs $$3$$ metres for every $$4$$ metres Bridgit runs. When Anna finishes, Bridgit has run $$84$$ metres, so that at that time Carol has run $$\frac{3}{4}\times 84$$ metres $$=63$$ metres. Hence Carol finishes $$\left( 100-63 \right)$$ metres $$= 37$$ metres behind Anna.
content.solution_plain: Carol finishes 25 metres behind Bridgit, so she travels 75 metres while Bridgit runs 100 metres. Therefore she runs 3 metres for every 4 metres Bridgit runs. When Anna finishes, Bridgit has run 84 metres, so that at that time Carol has run 3 4 84 metres =63 metres. Hence Carol finishes ( 100-63 ) metres = 37 metres behind Anna.

step_outline: Carol finishes $$25$$ metres behind Bridgit, so she travels $$75$$ metres while Bridgit runs $$100$$ metres.
Therefore she runs $$3$$ metres for every $$4$$ metres Bridgit runs.
When Anna finishes, Bridgit has run $$84$$ metres, so that at that time Carol has run $$\frac{3}{4}\times 84$$ metres $$=63$$ metres.
Hence Carol finishes $$\left( 100-63 \right)$$ metres $$= 37$$ metres behind Anna.

(D) 80
content.problem_plain: Tom and Jerry have to mail 2000 envelopes for a new marketing campaign. Jerry can mail 120 envelopes per hour. With Tom s help, they can get the job done in 10 hours. How long will it take Tom to finish the job by himself? (A) 24 (B) 25 (C) 30 (D) 80

content.problem_latex:
Isabella must take four $100$-point tests in her math class. Her goal is to achieve an average grade of $95$ on the tests. Her first two test scores were $97$ and $91$. After seeing her score on the third test, she realized she can still reach her goal. What is the lowest possible score she would have made on the third test?
(A) 90
(B) 92
(C) 95
(D) 96
(E) 97
content.problem_plain: Isabella must take four 100 -point tests in her math class. Her goal is to achieve an average grade of 95 on the tests. Her first two test scores were 97 and 91 . After seeing her score on the third test, she realized she can still reach her goal. What is the lowest possible score she would have made on the third test? (A) 90 (B) 92 (C) 95 (D) 96 (E) 97
content.solution_latex: Isabella wants an average grade of $95$ on her $4$ test; this also means that she wants the sum of her test scores to be at least $95\times 4=380$ (if she goes over this number, she\textquotesingle ll be over the goal!). She\textquotesingle s already taken two tests, which sum to $97+91=188$, which means she needs $380-188=192$ more points to achieve her desired average. In order to minimize the score on the third test, we assume that Isabella will receive all $100$ points on the fourth test. Therefore, the lowest score on the third test would be $192-100=92$.

(D) Friday

content.problem_plain: In a certain year, June 24 th ~is Thursday. What day is August 27 th~ in this year? (A) Monday (B) Tuesday (C) Thursday (D) Friday (E) Saturday
content.solution_latex: Counting from June $$24$$\textsuperscript{th}, after $30-24=6$ days it is June $$30$$\textsuperscript{st}. After $58$ days it is August $$27$$\textsuperscript{th}. In total, there are $6+58=64$ days. $64\div 7 =9R1$, which means August $$27$$\textsuperscript{th~}is Friday.

(E) 202
content.problem_plain: Six rectangles each with a common base width of 2 have lengths of 1,4,9,16,25 , and 36 . What is the sum of the areas of the six rectangles? (2014 AMC 8, Question #6) (A) 91 (B) 93 (C) 162 (D) 182 (E) 202
content.solution_latex: The sum of the areas is equal to $2 \cdot 1+2 \cdot 4+2 \cdot 9+2 \cdot 16+2 \cdot 25+2 \cdot 36$. This is equal to $2(1+4+9+16+25+36)$, which is equal to $2 \cdot 91$. This is equal to our final answer of (D) 182 .

content.problem_latex:
John is 33 years old. His three sons are 5, 6 and 10 years old. In how many years will the three sons together be as old as their father?
(A) 4
(B) 6
(C) 8
(D) 10
(E) 12
content.problem_plain: John is 33 years old. His three sons are 5, 6 and 10 years old. In how many years will the three sons together be as old as their father? (A) 4 (B) 6 (C) 8 (D) 10 (E) 12

content.problem_latex:
Jack bought a batch of goods that he hoped to earn a $$50\textbackslash\%$$ profit after selling them. On the first day, he sold $$60\textbackslash\%$$ of his goods at a profit of $$50\textbackslash\%$$. On the second day, he sold all remaining goods for a discount. If the total profit is $$76\textbackslash\%$$ of the planned profit, what was the discount rate?
(A) 20\textbackslash\%
(B) 21\textbackslash\%
(C) 22\textbackslash\%
(D) 24\textbackslash\%
content.problem_plain: Jack bought a batch of goods that he hoped to earn a 50 % profit after selling them. On the first day, he sold 60 % of his goods at a profit of 50 % . On the second day, he sold all remaining goods for a discount. If the total profit is 76 % of the planned profit, what was the discount rate? (A) 20 % (B) 21 % (C) 22 % (D) 24 %
content.solution_latex: Let the cost for the batch of goods be $$m$$ dollars.  Jack finally earned: $$m\times 50\textbackslash\%\times 92\textbackslash\%=0.38m$$.  On the first day he earned: $$m\times 60\textbackslash\%\times 50\textbackslash\%=0.30m$$.  Profit for the second day: $$0.38m-0.30m=0.08m$$.  Profit rate for the second day: $$\frac{0.08m}{\left( m-0.6m \right)}=20\textbackslash\%$$.  Percentage discount: $$1-\frac{\left( 1+20\textbackslash\% \right)}{\left( 1+50\textbackslash\% \right)}=20\textbackslash\%$$.
content.solution_plain: Let the cost for the batch of goods be m dollars. Jack finally earned: m 50 % 92 %=0.38m . On the first day he earned: m 60 % 50 %=0.30m . Profit for the second day: 0.38m-0.30m=0.08m . Profit rate for the second day: 0.08m ( m-0.6m ) =20 % . Percentage discount: 1- ( 1+20 % ) ( 1+50 % ) =20 % .

Jack finally earned: $$m\times 50\textbackslash\%\times 92\textbackslash\%=0.38m$$.
On the first day he earned: $$m\times 60\textbackslash\%\times 50\textbackslash\%=0.30m$$.
Profit for the second day: $$0.38m-0.30m=0.08m$$.
Profit rate for the second day: $$\frac{0.08m}{\left( m-0.6m \right)}=20\textbackslash\%$$.
Percentage discount: $$1-\frac{\left( 1+20\textbackslash\% \right)}{\left( 1+50\textbackslash\% \right)}=20\textbackslash\%$$.

Grandpa suggested dividing all the peanuts between the family members in the following way: one person would get $5$ kilos, two people would get $4$ kilos each, four people would get $2$ kilos each, one person would get $6$ kilos, and one person would not get any peanuts. (A) 3
(B) 4
(C) 5
(D) 6
(E) 7
content.problem_plain: Grandpa suggested dividing all the peanuts between the family members in the following way: one person would get 5 kilos, two people would get 4 kilos each, four people would get 2 kilos each, one person would get 6 kilos, and one person would not get any peanuts. Anna is $$10$$ years old and the age of her mother is $$4$$ times that of Anna. How old will Anna\textquotesingle s mother be when Anna\textquotesingle s mother is twice as old as Anna? (Math kangaroo Problem, Level $$5-6$$)
(A) 40
(B) 50
(C) 60
(D) 70
(E) 80
content.problem_plain: Anna is 10 years old and the age of her mother is 4 times that of Anna. How old will Anna s mother be when Anna s mother is twice as old as Anna? (Math kangaroo Problem, Level 5-6 ) (A) 40 (B) 50 (C) 60 (D) 70 (E) 80
content.solution_latex: The age difference between them is $(4-1)\times10=30$, which stays the same forever. Then, when Anna\textquotesingle s mother is twice as old as Anna, Anna should be $30$ years old, then her mom should be $30\times2=60$.
content.solution_plain: The age difference between them is (4-1) 10=30 , which stays the same forever. Then, when Anna s mother is twice as old as Anna, Anna should be 30 years old, then her mom should be 30 2=60 .

content.problem_latex:
The February of a given year has five Fridays. What day of the week was January $$31$$\textsuperscript{th~}of that year?
(A) Friday
(B) Saturday
(C) Wednesday
(D) Thursday
content.problem_plain: The February of a given year has five Fridays. What day of the week was January 31 th~ of that year? (A) Friday (B) Saturday (C) Wednesday (D) Thursday
content.solution_latex: There are seven days in a week. In order to have five Fridays, a month has at least $$4\times 7+1=29$$ days. Since February has at most $$29$$ days, the $$1$$\textsuperscript{st} and $$29$$\textsuperscript{th} days of this February must be Friday, which means that January $$31$$ is Thursday.
content.solution_plain: There are seven days in a week. In order to have five Fridays, a month has at least 4 7+1=29 days. Since February has at most 29 days, the 1 st and 29 th days of this February must be Friday, which means that January 31 is Thursday.

step_outline: There are seven days in a week.
In order to have five Fridays, a month has at least $$4\times 7+1=29$$ days.
Since February has at most $$29$$ days, the $$1$$\textsuperscript{st} and $$29$$\textsuperscript{th} days of this February must be Friday, which means that January $$31$$ is Thursday.

content.problem_latex:
George takes 4 minutes to go from 1st floor to 3rd floor. He just realised that he forgot his water bottle on the 5th floor. He is now on the 2nd floor. How long would it take him to get his watter bottle?
(A) 4 minutes
(B) 5 minutes
(C) 6 minutes
(D) 8 minutes
(E) None of the above.
content.problem_plain: George takes 4 minutes to go from 1st floor to 3rd floor. He just realised that he forgot his water bottle on the 5th floor. He is now on the 2nd floor. How long would it take him to get his watter bottle? (A) 4 minutes (B) 5 minutes (C) 6 minutes (D) 8 minutes (E) None of the above.
content.solution_latex: 2 gaps between 1st to 3rd floor. hence, 4/2=2 minutes per gap.  Moving from 2nd to 5th floor, 3 gaps -\textgreater{} 3 gaps x 2 minutes per gap = 6 minutes.

content.problem_plain: At which of~ these times is the angle between the minute hand and the hour hand of a clock equal to 150^ ? (A) 9 pm (B) 8 pm (C) 6 pm (D) 5 pm (E) 4 pm
content.solution_latex: At all the times given, the minute hand is pointing to $$12$$. When the minute hand is pointing to $$12$$ and the angle between the hands is $$150^{}\circ$$, the hour hand has turned $$\frac{150}{360}= \frac{5}{12}$$ of a complete turn. Therefore the hour hand will point at $$5$$ and the time will be $$5\text{pm}$$. (There are other times when the angle between the hands is $$150^{}\circ$$ but, of these, only at $$7\text{pm}$$ does the minute hand point to $$12$$ and $$7\text{pm}$$ is not one of the times given.)
content.solution_plain: At all the times given, the minute hand is pointing to 12 . When the minute hand is pointing to 12 and the angle between the hands is 150^ , the hour hand has turned 150 360 = 5 12 of a complete turn. Therefore the hour hand will point at 5 and the time will be 5 pm . (There are other times when the angle between the hands is 150^ but, of these, only at 7 pm does the minute hand point to 12 and 7 pm is not one of the times given.)

step_outline: At all the times given, the minute hand is pointing to $$12$$.
When the minute hand is pointing to $$12$$ and the angle between the hands is $$150^{}\circ$$, the hour hand has turned $$\frac{150}{360}= \frac{5}{12}$$ of a complete turn.
Therefore the hour hand will point at $$5$$ and the time will be $$5\text{pm}$$.

(There are other times when the angle between the hands is $$150^{}\circ$$ but, of these, only at $$7\text{pm}$$ does the minute hand point to $$12$$ and $$7\text{pm}$$ is not one of the times given.)

content.problem_latex:
Lisa is buying potatoes at a grocery store. She can either spend $10$ dollars on a $25\text{-lb}$ bag or $15$ dollars on a $35\text{-lb}$ bag. Whic is the cheaper one?
(A) The $25\text{-lb}$ bag
(B) The $35\text{-lb}$ bag
content.problem_plain: Lisa is buying potatoes at a grocery store. She can either spend 10 dollars on a 25 -lb bag or 15 dollars on a 35 -lb bag. Whic is the cheaper one? (A) The 25 -lb bag (B) The 35 -lb bag

(B) 108
(C) 216
(D) 360
content.problem_plain: Tom, Joanna and Jacky each come up with a number. The sum of the three numbers is 360 . Tom s number is twice Joanna s number, and Joanna s number is three times Jacky s number. What are these three numbers? (A) 36 (B) 108 (C) 216 (D) 360

Jacky\textquotesingle s number: $$360 \div (6 +3+1) =36$$. Joanna\textquotesingle s number: $$36 \times 3=108$$. Tom\textquotesingle s number: $$36 \times 6 =216$$.

content.problem_latex:
( $$2004$$ AMC $$8$$ Problem, Question \#$$12$$)  Niki usually leaves her cell phone on. If her cell phone is on but she is not actually using it, the battery will last for $$24$$ hours. If she is using it constantly, the battery will last for only $$3$$ hours. Since the last recharge, her phone has been on $$9$$ hours, and during that time she has used it for $$60$$ minutes. If she doesn\textquotesingle t use it any more but leaves the phone on, how many more hours will the battery last?
(A) 7
(B) 8
(C) 11
(D) 14
(E) 15
content.problem_plain: ( 2004 AMC 8 Problem, Question # 12 ) Niki usually leaves her cell phone on. If her cell phone is on but she is not actually using it, the battery will last for 24 hours. If she is using it constantly, the battery will last for only 3 hours. Since the last recharge, her phone has been on 9 hours, and during that time she has used it for 60 minutes. If she doesn t use it any more but leaves the phone on, how many more hours will the battery last? (A) 7 (B) 8 (C) 11 (D) 14 (E) 15
content.solution_latex: When not being used, the cell phone uses up $$\dfrac{1}{24}$$ of its battery per hour. When being used, the cell phone uses up $$\dfrac{1}{3}$$ of its battery per hour. Since Niki\textquotesingle s phone has been on for $$9$$ hours, of those $$8$$ simply on and~~being used to talk, $$8\left(\dfrac{1}{24}\right)+1\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)=\dfrac{2}{3}$$ of its battery has been used up. To drain the remaining $$\dfrac{1}{3}$$ the phone can last for $$\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{3}}{\dfrac{1}{24}}=\boxed{(\text{B})8}$$ more hours without being used.

To drain the remaining 1 3 the phone can last for 1 3 1 24 = ( B )8 more hours without being used.

(D) 7

content.problem_plain: A school bought 12 volleyballs and basketballs in total for ＄ 340 . Each basketball costs ＄ 30 . Each volleyball costs ＄ 25 . How many basketballs did the school buy? (A) 4 (B) 5 (C) 6 (D) 7 (E) 8
content.solution_latex: If all were basketballs,  $$12\times $$＄$$30=$$＄$$360$$  the total cost would be ＄$$360$$.  ＄$$360-$$＄$$340=$$＄$$20$$  There is a difference of ＄$$20$$.  ＄$$30-$$＄$$25=$$＄$$5$$  ＄$$20\div $$＄$$5=4$$  $$12-4=8$$  The school bought $$8$$ basketballs.

content.problem_latex:
There is a tournament at the pool, First, $$13$$ children signed up and then another $$19$$ children signed up, Six teams with an equal number of members each are needed for the tournament, At least how many more children need to sign up so that,the six teams can be formed . (2017 Math Kangaroo Problem, Level 3-4, Question \#13)
(A) 1
(B) 2
(C) 3
(D) 4
(E) 5
content.problem_plain: There is a tournament at the pool, First, 13 children signed up and then another 19 children signed up, Six teams with an equal number of members each are needed for the tournament, At least how many more children need to sign up so that,the six teams can be formed . (2017 Math Kangaroo Problem, Level 3-4, Question #13) (A) 1 (B) 2 (C) 3 (D) 4 (E) 5
content.solution_latex: The total number of children is $$13+19=32$$, To form six teams with the same number of children in each team, we divide $$32$$ by $$6$$, $$32\div6=5$$$$\rm R$$$$2$$, The remaining $$2$$ children will need $$4$$ more children to form the sixth team.

content.problem_latex:
There are $$15$$ balls in a box: white balls, red balls and black balls. The number of white balls is $$7$$ times greater than the number of red balls. How many black balls are there in the box? ($$1998$$ Math Kangaroo Problem, Level $$3-4$$, Question \#$$16$$)
(A) 1
(B) 3
(C) 5
(D) 7
(E) 9
content.problem_plain: There are 15 balls in a box: white balls, red balls and black balls. The number of white balls is 7 times greater than the number of red balls. How many black balls are there in the box? ( 1998 Math Kangaroo Problem, Level 3-4 , Question # 16 ) (A) 1 (B) 3 (C) 5 (D) 7 (E) 9
content.solution_latex: The numbers of white balls and red balls in total must be a multiple of $7+1=8$, so from $1$ to $15$, only $8$ itself can match the condition. Thus, there are $15-8=7$ black balls in the box.

(B) 40

(B) 400
(C) 450
(D) 600
(E) 900

content.problem_latex:
Cagney can frost a cupcake every $$20$$ seconds and Lacey can frost a cupcake every $$30$$ seconds. Working together, how many cupcakes can they frost in $$5$$ minutes? ($$2012$$ AMC $$10\rm A$$ Problem, Question \#$$11$$)
(A) 10
(B) 15
(C) 20
(D) 25
(E) 30
content.problem_plain: Cagney can frost a cupcake every 20 seconds and Lacey can frost a cupcake every 30 seconds. Working together, how many cupcakes can they frost in 5 minutes? ( 2012 AMC 10 A Problem, Question # 11 ) (A) 10 (B) 15 (C) 20 (D) 25 (E) 30
content.solution_latex: Method1: Cagney can frost one in $$20$$ seconds, and Lacey can frost one in $$30$$ seconds. Working together, they can frost one in $$\dfrac{20\cdot30}{20+30}=\dfrac{600}{50}=12$$ seconds. In $$300$$ seconds ( $$5$$ minutes), they can frost $$\boxed{(\text{D})25}$$ cupcakes.  Method2: In $$300$$ seconds ($$5$$ minutes), Cagney will frost $$\dfrac{300}{20}=15$$ cupcakes, and Lacey will frost $$\dfrac{300}{30}=10$$ ~cupcakes. Therefore, working together they will frost $$15+10=\boxed{(\text{D})25}$$ cupcakes.  Method3: Since Cagney frosts $$3$$ cupcakes a minute, and Lacey frosts $$2$$ cupcakes a minute, they together frost $$3+2=5$$ cupcakes a minute. Therefore, in $$5$$ minutes, they frost $$5\times5=25\Rightarrow\boxed{(\text{D})}$$.

content.problem_plain: Anna, Bridgit and Carol run in a 100 m race. When Anna finishes, Bridgit is 16 m behind her and when Bridgit finishes, Carol is 25 m behind her. The girls run at constant speeds throughout the race. How far behind was Carol when Anna finished? (A) 37 m (B) 41 m (C) 50 m (D) 55 m (E) 60 m
content.solution_latex: Carol finishes $$25$$ metres behind Bridgit, so she travels $$75$$ metres while Bridgit runs $$100$$ metres. Therefore she runs $$3$$ metres for every $$4$$ metres Bridgit runs. When Anna finishes, Bridgit has run $$84$$ metres, so that at that time Carol has run $$\frac{3}{4}\times 84$$ metres $$=63$$ metres. Hence Carol finishes $$\left( 100-63 \right)$$ metres $$= 37$$ metres behind Anna.
content.solution_plain: Carol finishes 25 metres behind Bridgit, so she travels 75 metres while Bridgit runs 100 metres. Therefore she runs 3 metres for every 4 metres Bridgit runs. When Anna finishes, Bridgit has run 84 metres, so that at that time Carol has run 3 4 84 metres =63 metres. Hence Carol finishes ( 100-63 ) metres = 37 metres behind Anna.

content.problem_plain: Geraint always cycles to work, leaving at 8 am every morning. When he averages 15 km / h ; ,he arrives 10 minutes late. However, when he averages 30 km / h ; , he arrives 10 minutes early. What speed should he average to arrive on time? (A) 20 km / h ; (B) 21 km / h ; (C) 22.5 km / h ; (D) 24 km / h ; (E) 25 km / h ;
content.solution_latex: Let $$x\text{km}$$ be the distance Geraint cycles and let $$t $$ hours be the time his journey should take if he is to be on time. Since $$\frac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}}\text{=time}$$, the information in the question tells us that  $$\frac{x}{15}=t+\frac{1}{6}$$ and that $$\frac{x}{30}=t-\frac{1}{6}$$. When we subtract the second equation from the first, we obtain  $$\frac{x}{30}=\frac{2}{6}$$ and so $$x = 10$$. Hence, from the second equation, $$\frac{10}{30}=t-\frac{1}{6}$$ and so $$t=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{1}{2}$$. Therefore, to arrive on time, Geraint needs to travel $$10\text{km}$$ in~ $$\frac{1}{2}$$ hour, which is an average speed of $$20{\text{km}}/{\text{h}}\textbackslash;$$.
content.solution_plain: Let x km be the distance Geraint cycles and let t hours be the time his journey should take if he is to be on time. Since distance speed =time , the information in the question tells us that x 15 =t+ 1 6 and that x 30 =t- 1 6 . When we subtract the second equation from the first, we obtain x 30 = 2 6 and so x = 10 . Hence, from the second equation, 10 30 =t- 1 6 and so t= 1 3 + 1 6 = 1 2 . Therefore, to arrive on time, Geraint needs to travel 10 km in~ 1 2 hour, which is an average speed of 20 km / h ; .

When we subtract the second equation from the first, we obtain  $$\frac{x}{30}=\frac{2}{6}$$ and so $$x = 10$$.
Hence, from the second equation, $$\frac{10}{30}=t-\frac{1}{6}$$ and so $$t=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{1}{2}$$.
Therefore, to arrive on time, Geraint needs to travel $$10\text{km}$$ in~ $$\frac{1}{2}$$ hour, which is an average speed of $$20{\text{km}}/{\text{h}}\textbackslash;$$.

Daniel raises some ducks and dogs. All the ducks and dogs have $18$ legs and $5$ pairs of wings in total. If all the ducks and dogs each lifts one leg off the ground, they lift~\uline{~~~~~~~~~~}~legs in total.
(A) 18
(B) 13
(C) 9
(D) 7

content.problem_plain: Daniel raises some ducks and dogs. All the ducks and dogs have 18 legs and 5 pairs of wings in total. If all the ducks and dogs each lifts one leg off the ground, they lift~ ~~~~~~~~~~ ~legs in total. (A) 18 (B) 13 (C) 9 (D) 7 (E) 5
content.solution_latex: $5$ pairs of wings means there are $5$ ducks, so there are $18-5\times2=8$ legs for dogs, which is $8\div4=2$. Thus, there are $2+5=7$ animals and they will lift $7$ legs.

content.problem_latex:
Water from the first faucet fills the swimming pool in $18$ hours. Water from each of the two other faucets fills the same swimming pool $4$ times faster. In how many hours will the swimming pool be filled if all three faucets are opened?
(A) 1
(B) 2
(C) 3
(D) 4
(E) 5
content.problem_plain: Water from the first faucet fills the swimming pool in 18 hours. Water from each of the two other faucets fills the same swimming pool 4 times faster. In how many hours will the swimming pool be filled if all three faucets are opened? (A) 1 (B) 2 (C) 3 (D) 4 (E) 5
content.solution_latex: The efficiency of the first faucet is $\frac1{18}$ and that of the other two is $\frac29$.  Thus it takes $1\div (\frac1{18}+\frac29\times2)=2$ hours to fill the pool.
content.solution_plain: The efficiency of the first faucet is 1 18 and that of the other two is 29 . Thus it takes 1 ( 1 18 + 29 2)=2 hours to fill the pool.

step_outline: The efficiency of the first faucet is $\frac1{18}$ and that of the other two is $\frac29$.
Thus it takes $1\div (\frac1{18}+\frac29\times2)=2$ hours to fill the pool.

content.problem_plain: Annie usually leaves her cell phone on. If her cell phone is on but she is not actually using it, the battery will last for 36 hours. If she is using it constantly, the battery will last for only 10 hours. Since the last recharge, her phone has been on 20 hours, and during that time she has used it for 120 minutes. If she only keeps using the phone 3 hours more and then leaves the phone on, how many more hours will the battery last after she leaves the phone on? (Adapted from 2004 AMC 8 Problem, Question # 12 ) (A) 0 (B) 1 (C) 3 (D) 4 (E) 5
content.solution_latex: When not being used, the cell phone uses up $$\dfrac{1}{36}$$ of its battery per hour. When being used, the cell phone uses up $$\dfrac{1}{10}$$ of its battery per hour. Since Annie\textquotesingle s phone has been on for $$20$$ hours, of those $$18$$ simply on and $2$ being used, $$18\times\left(\dfrac{1}{36}\right)+2\times\left(\dfrac{1}{10}\right)=\dfrac{7}{10}$$ of its battery has been used up. To drain the remaining $$\dfrac{3}{10}$$, the phone can last for $$(\frac{3}{10}-3\times\frac1{10})\div \frac1{36}=0$$ more hours without being used.
content.solution_plain: When not being used, the cell phone uses up 1 36 of its battery per hour. When being used, the cell phone uses up 1 10 of its battery per hour. Since Annie s phone has been on for 20 hours, of those 18 simply on and 2 being used, 18 ( 1 36 )+2 ( 1 10 )= 7 10 of its battery has been used up. To drain the remaining 3 10 , the phone can last for ( 3 10 -3 1 10 ) 1 36 =0 more hours without being used.

When being used, the cell phone uses up $$\dfrac{1}{10}$$ of its battery per hour.

Since Annie\textquotesingle s phone has been on for $$20$$ hours, of those $$18$$ simply on and $2$ being used, $$18\times\left(\dfrac{1}{36}\right)+2\times\left(\dfrac{1}{10}\right)=\dfrac{7}{10}$$ of its battery has been used up.
To drain the remaining $$\dfrac{3}{10}$$, the phone can last for $$(\frac{3}{10}-3\times\frac1{10})\div \frac1{36}=0$$ more hours without being used.

content.problem_latex:
A teacher distributes scorecards to students, if everyone gets $3$ cards, there will be a shortage of $12$ cards. If everyone gets $2$ cards, all these cards will just be divided. Then, how many students are there in total?
(A) 4
(B) 6
(C) 10
(D) 12
content.problem_plain: A teacher distributes scorecards to students, if everyone gets 3 cards, there will be a shortage of 12 cards. If everyone gets 2 cards, all these cards will just be divided. Then, how many students are there in total? (A) 4 (B) 6 (C) 10 (D) 12
content.solution_latex: If $2-1=1$ less card is given to students, the situation would transfer from \textquotesingle a shortage of $12$ cards\textquotesingle~ to \textquotesingle all cards are just divided\textquotesingle{} . Therefore. the shortage of $12$ cards is equal to the number of cards that everyone gets $1$ less card. So, there are in total $12\div1=12$ students.

content.problem_plain: Karl Lecter has been collecting 1 p , 2 p and 5 p coins in a jar. All but 10 of his coins are 1 p coins, all but 10 are 2 p coins, and all but 10 are 5 p coins. How much money does he have? (A) 8 p (B) 10 p (C) 25 p (D) 40 p (E) 80 p
content.solution_latex: All but $$10$$ of the coins are $$1\text{p}$$ coins, which tells us that the number of $$2\text{p}$$ and $$5\text{p}$$ coins adds up to $$10$$. Similarly, the total of the $$1\text{p}$$ and $$5\text{p}$$ coins is $$10$$ and the total of the $$1\text{p}$$ and $$2\text{p}$$ coins is $$10$$. He therefore has $$5$$ of each coin, giving a total of $$(5 \times1)+ (5 \times2)+(5\times5)=40\text{p}$$.
content.solution_plain: All but 10 of the coins are 1 p coins, which tells us that the number of 2 p and 5 p coins adds up to 10 . Similarly, the total of the 1 p and 5 p coins is 10 and the total of the 1 p and 2 p coins is 10 . He therefore has 5 of each coin, giving a total of (5 1)+ (5 2)+(5 5)=40 p .

step_outline: All but $$10$$ of the coins are $$1\text{p}$$ coins, which tells us that the number of $$2\text{p}$$ and $$5\text{p}$$ coins adds up to $$10$$.
Similarly, the total of the $$1\text{p}$$ and $$5\text{p}$$ coins is $$10$$ and the total of the $$1\text{p}$$ and $$2\text{p}$$ coins is $$10$$.
He therefore has $$5$$ of each coin, giving a total of $$(5 \times1)+ (5 \times2)+(5\times5)=40\text{p}$$.

If $$28$$ January $$2000$$ falls on a Friday, which day of the week will $$28$$ January $2001$ fall on?
(A) Thursday
(B) Friday
(C) Saturday
(D) Sunday
(E) Monday
content.problem_plain: If 28 January 2000 falls on a Friday, which day of the week will 28 January 2001 fall on? (A) Thursday (B) Friday (C) Saturday (D) Sunday (E) Monday
content.solution_latex: $2000$ is a leap year and the period includes $29$ Feb, so there are $366$ days.  $366\div7=52$ R $2$  Friday $+$ $2$ days $\rightarrow$ Sunday

content.problem_plain: Anna, Bridgit and Carol run in a 100 m race. When Anna finishes, Bridgit is 16 m behind her and when Bridgit finishes, Carol is 25 m behind her. The girls run at constant speeds throughout the race. How far behind was Carol when Anna finished? (A) 37 m (B) 41 m (C) 50 m (D) 55 m (E) 60 m
content.solution_latex: Carol finishes $$25$$ metres behind Bridgit, so she travels $$75$$ metres while Bridgit runs $$100$$ metres. Therefore she runs $$3$$ metres for every $$4$$ metres Bridgit runs. When Anna finishes, Bridgit has run $$84$$ metres, so that at that time Carol has run $$\frac{3}{4}\times 84$$ metres $$=63$$ metres. Hence Carol finishes $$\left( 100-63 \right)$$ metres $$= 37$$ metres behind Anna.
content.solution_plain: Carol finishes 25 metres behind Bridgit, so she travels 75 metres while Bridgit runs 100 metres. Therefore she runs 3 metres for every 4 metres Bridgit runs. When Anna finishes, Bridgit has run 84 metres, so that at that time Carol has run 3 4 84 metres =63 metres. Hence Carol finishes ( 100-63 ) metres = 37 metres behind Anna.

step_outline: Carol finishes $$25$$ metres behind Bridgit, so she travels $$75$$ metres while Bridgit runs $$100$$ metres.
Therefore she runs $$3$$ metres for every $$4$$ metres Bridgit runs.
When Anna finishes, Bridgit has run $$84$$ metres, so that at that time Carol has run $$\frac{3}{4}\times 84$$ metres $$=63$$ metres.
Hence Carol finishes $$\left( 100-63 \right)$$ metres $$= 37$$ metres behind Anna.

content.problem_latex:
If $$6$$ cans of Sweet Stuff Soda all together contain $$96$$ teaspoons of sugar, then there are a total of teaspoons of sugar in $$15$$ cans.
(A) 192
(B) 208
(C) 240
(D) 288
content.problem_plain: If 6 cans of Sweet Stuff Soda all together contain 96 teaspoons of sugar, then there are a total of teaspoons of sugar in 15 cans. (A) 192 (B) 208 (C) 240 (D) 288
content.solution_latex: If $$6$$ cans contain $$96$$ teaspoons of sugar, $$1$$ can contains $$96\div6 = 16$$ teaspoons of sugar. Thus $$15$$ cans contain $$16\times15 =240$$ teaspoons of sugar.
content.solution_plain: If 6 cans contain 96 teaspoons of sugar, 1 can contains 96 6 = 16 teaspoons of sugar. Thus 15 cans contain 16 15 =240 teaspoons of sugar.

content.problem_plain: Paul was going to buy 4 servings of ice cream, but he was 80 cents short. So, he bought 3 servings and had 30 cents left. What was the price of one serving of ice cream? (A) 70 cents (B) 80 cents (C) 90 cents (D) 1 dollar (E) 1 dollar 10 cents

content.problem_latex:
In a verbal test, the average score of $$40$$ students is $$25$$. The lowest score is $$5$$. At most how many student(s) can get $$100$$ points?
(A) 11
(B) 10
(C) 9
(D) 8
(E) 7
content.problem_plain: In a verbal test, the average score of 40 students is 25 . The lowest score is 5 . At most how many student(s) can get 100 points? (A) 11 (B) 10 (C) 9 (D) 8 (E) 7
content.solution_latex: Total points: $$40\times 25=1000$$.  To have as many $100$ points as possible, we should consider there are only two groups of students: everyone gets $5$ points in a group and everyone gets $100$ in the other group.  $$10\times 100=1000$$, so there must be less than $10$ people getting $100$ points.  If there are nine $100$s: $$9\times 100+31\times 5=1055\textgreater1000$$, which is impossible.  If there are eight $100$s:$$8\times 100+32\times 5=960\textless{}1000$$.  Thus, the answer is $$8$$.
content.solution_plain: Total points: 40 25=1000 . To have as many 100 points as possible, we should consider there are only two groups of students: everyone gets 5 points in a group and everyone gets 100 in the other group. 10 100=1000 , so there must be less than 10 people getting 100 points. If there are nine 100 s: 9 100+31 5=1055 1000 , which is impossible. If there are eight 100 s: 8 100+32 5=960 1000 . Thus, the answer is 8 .

Thus, the answer is $$8$$.

Arrange $$28$$ balls to form a square.  One ball is placed at each corner of the square.  How many balls are there on each side of the square?~\uline{~~~~~~~~~~}~

content.problem_latex:
A total of $$20$$ chickens and rabbits are caged together. If there are $$56$$ legs in total, how many chickens are there in the cage?
(A) 20
(B) 16
(C) 15
(D) 12
(E) 10
content.problem_plain: A total of 20 chickens and rabbits are caged together. If there are 56 legs in total, how many chickens are there in the cage? (A) 20 (B) 16 (C) 15 (D) 12 (E) 10
content.solution_latex: Suppose the number of chickens is $x$, so the number of rabbits is $(20-x)$.  $2x+(20-x)\times4=56$, $x=12$.

content.problem_latex:
Students guess that Norb\textquotesingle s age is $$24$$, $$28$$, $$30$$, $$32$$, $$36$$, $$38$$, $$41$$, $$44$$, $$47$$, and $$49$$. Norb says, "At least half of you guessed too low, two of you are off by one, and my age is a prime number." How old is Norb? ($$2011$$ AMC $$8$$ problem, Question \#$$21$$)
(A) 29
(B) 31
(C) 37
(D) 43
(E) 48
content.problem_plain: Students guess that Norb s age is 24 , 28 , 30 , 32 , 36 , 38 , 41 , 44 , 47 , and 49 . Norb says, "At least half of you guessed too low, two of you are off by one, and my age is a prime number." How old is Norb? ( 2011 AMC 8 problem, Question # 21 ) (A) 29 (B) 31 (C) 37 (D) 43 (E) 48
content.solution_latex: If at least half the guesses are too low, then Norb\textquotesingle s age must be greater than $$36$$.  If two of the guesses are off by one, then his age is in between two guesses whose difference is $$2$$. It could be $$31$$, $$37$$, or $$48$$, but because his age is greater than $$36$$, it can only be $$37$$ or $$48$$.  Lastly, Norb\textquotesingle s age is a prime number so the answer must be $$37$$.
content.solution_plain: If at least half the guesses are too low, then Norb s age must be greater than 36 . If two of the guesses are off by one, then his age is in between two guesses whose difference is 2 . It could be 31 , 37 , or 48 , but because his age is greater than 36 , it can only be 37 or 48 . Lastly, Norb s age is a prime number so the answer must be 37 .

Lastly, Norb\textquotesingle s age is a prime number so the answer must be $$37$$.

content.problem_latex:
On a map of England and Wales the distance between St Ives in Cornwall and St Ives in Cambridgeshire measures $$18\text{cm}$$. In reality the distance between the two towns is about $$450\text{km}$$.  Which of the options below is the scale of the map?
(A) 1:2500000
(B) 1:1000000
(C) 1:750000
(D) 1:500000
(E) 1:250000
content.problem_plain: On a map of England and Wales the distance between St Ives in Cornwall and St Ives in Cambridgeshire measures 18 cm . In reality the distance between the two towns is about 450 km . Which of the options below is the scale of the map? (A) 1:2500000 (B) 1:1000000 (C) 1:750000 (D) 1:500000 (E) 1:250000
content.solution_latex: The scale of the map is $$18\text{cm} : 450\text{km}$$; converting the actual distance to centimetres, this is equivalent to $$18 : 450 \times 1000 \times 100 = 18 : 45 000 000$$. Simplifying gives a scale of $$1 : 2 500 000$$.
content.solution_plain: The scale of the map is 18 cm : 450 km ; converting the actual distance to centimetres, this is equivalent to 18 : 450 1000 100 = 18 : 45 000 000 . Simplifying gives a scale of 1 : 2 500 000 .

The five-digit number $411A2$ is a multiple of $9$. What digit does A represent?
(A) 0
(B) 1
(C) 2
(D) 3
(E) None of the above
content.problem_plain: The five-digit number 411A2 is a multiple of 9 . What digit does A represent? (A) 0 (B) 1 (C) 2 (D) 3 (E) None of the above
content.solution_latex: From the Divisibility Rule of $9$, the sum of the digit of 411A2 must be divisible by $9$.  $4 + 1 + 1+ 4 + 2 = 8 + A$  Hence A = $1$.

content.problem_latex:
Some students are going to an amusement park. If each student carries $9$ bottles of water in his or her backpack, there will be $2$ students carrying nothing; if one of the students carries $2$ bottles of water and the rest students carry $8$ bottles each, all the bottles of water can be carried to the park. There are~\uline{~~~~~~~~~~}~bottles of water in total that need to be carried.
(A) 80
(B) 90
(C) 92
(D) 102
content.problem_plain: Some students are going to an amusement park. If each student carries 9 bottles of water in his or her backpack, there will be 2 students carrying nothing; if one of the students carries 2 bottles of water and the rest students carry 8 bottles each, all the bottles of water can be carried to the park. There are~ ~~~~~~~~~~ ~bottles of water in total that need to be carried. (A) 80 (B) 90 (C) 92 (D) 102
content.solution_latex: Solve the problem as a problem of surplus and shortage. If each student carries $9$ bottles, there will be a shortage of $9\times2=18$ bottles;  if each student carries $8$ bottles, there will be a shortage of $8-2=6$ bottles. Therefore, there are $(18-6)\div(9-8)=12$ students and $12\times9-18=90$ bottles of water.
content.solution_plain: Solve the problem as a problem of surplus and shortage. If each student carries 9 bottles, there will be a shortage of 9 2=18 bottles; if each student carries 8 bottles, there will be a shortage of 8-2=6 bottles. Therefore, there are (18-6) (9-8)=12 students and 12 9-18=90 bottles of water.

(D) Thursday

content.problem_plain: My mom s birthday is on Sunday, and my dad s birthday is 55 days later. On what day of the week will my dad s birthday be? ． (A) Sunday (B) Monday (C) Tuesday (D) Thursday (E) Saturday
content.solution_latex: After $$1$$ day, it is Monday; after $$2$$ days, it is Tuesday; after $$3$$ days, it is Wednesday $$\cdots$$ After $$8$$ days, it is still Monday, and the period is $$7$$ days. $$55\div 7=7$$(weeks) $$\rm R$$ $$6$$ (days). The birthday is Saturday.
content.solution_plain: After 1 day, it is Monday; after 2 days, it is Tuesday; after 3 days, it is Wednesday After 8 days, it is still Monday, and the period is 7 days. 55 7=7 (weeks) R 6 (days). The birthday is Saturday.

step_outline: After $$1$$ day, it is Monday; after $$2$$ days, it is Tuesday; after $$3$$ days, it is Wednesday $$\cdots$$ After $$8$$ days, it is still Monday, and the period is $$7$$ days.
The birthday is Saturday.

content.problem_plain: At which of these times is the angle between the minute hand and the hour hand of a clock equal to 150^ ? (A) 9 pm (B) 8 pm (C) 6 pm (D) 5 pm (E) 4 pm
content.solution_latex: At all the times given, the minute hand is pointing to $$12$$. When the minute hand is pointing to $$12$$ and the angle between the hands is $$150^{}\circ$$, the hour hand has turned $$\frac{150}{360}= \frac{5}{12}$$ of a complete turn. Therefore the hour hand will point at $$5$$ and the time will be $$5\text{pm}$$. (There are other times when the angle between the hands is $$150^{}\circ$$ but, of these, only at $$7\text{pm}$$ does the minute hand point to $$12$$ and $$7\text{pm}$$ is not one of the times given.)
content.solution_plain: At all the times given, the minute hand is pointing to 12 . When the minute hand is pointing to 12 and the angle between the hands is 150^ , the hour hand has turned 150 360 = 5 12 of a complete turn. Therefore the hour hand will point at 5 and the time will be 5 pm . (There are other times when the angle between the hands is 150^ but, of these, only at 7 pm does the minute hand point to 12 and 7 pm is not one of the times given.)

(There are other times when the angle between the hands is $$150^{}\circ$$ but, of these, only at $$7\text{pm}$$ does the minute hand point to $$12$$ and $$7\text{pm}$$ is not one of the times given.)

content.problem_latex:
Cici and Kiki took the same quiz. They agreed on the following rules: a person got $10$ points for a correct answer and lost $5$ points otherwise (getting a wrong answer or skipping a question). They each answered $10$ questions and together got $95$ points. If Cici got $15$ more points than Kiki, how many questions did Kiki answer correctly?
(A) 6
(B) 7
(C) 8
(D) 9
content.problem_plain: Cici and Kiki took the same quiz. They agreed on the following rules: a person got 10 points for a correct answer and lost 5 points otherwise (getting a wrong answer or skipping a question). They each answered 10 questions and together got 95 points. If Cici got 15 more points than Kiki, how many questions did Kiki answer correctly? (A) 6 (B) 7 (C) 8 (D) 9
content.solution_latex: Kiki got $(95-15)\div2=40$ points. If she answered all the questions correctly, she would have gotten $100$ points. Hence, she skipped or got $(100-40)\div(10+5)=4$ wrong answers and she answered $10-4=6$ questions correctly.
content.solution_plain: Kiki got (95-15) 2=40 points. If she answered all the questions correctly, she would have gotten 100 points. Hence, she skipped or got (100-40) (10+5)=4 wrong answers and she answered 10-4=6 questions correctly.

content.problem_latex:
Julian started reading a book with $$348$$ pages. He read $$6$$ pages on each day from Monday to Friday and $$11$$ pages on each day on Saturday and Sunday. He started reading on a Thursday. On which day of the week did he finish reading the book? ．
(A) Friday
(B) Saturday
(C) Sunday
(D) Monday
content.problem_plain: Julian started reading a book with 348 pages. He read 6 pages on each day from Monday to Friday and 11 pages on each day on Saturday and Sunday. He started reading on a Thursday. On which day of the week did he finish reading the book? ． (A) Friday (B) Saturday (C) Sunday (D) Monday
content.solution_latex: Thursday to Sunday $$\rightarrow 6\times 2+11\times 2=34$$ pages  $$348-34=314$$  $1$ week (Monday to Sunday) $$\rightarrow 11\times 2+6\times 5=52$$ pages  $6$ weeks $$\rightarrow 52\times6=312$$ pages  After reading another $312$ pages in $6$ weeks, Julian has another $(314 -- 312) = 2$ pages left to read. It will now be a Monday and Julian can finish reading the last $2$ pages.
content.solution_plain: Thursday to Sunday 6 2+11 2=34 pages 348-34=314 1 week (Monday to Sunday) 11 2+6 5=52 pages 6 weeks 52 6=312 pages After reading another 312 pages in 6 weeks, Julian has another (314 -- 312) = 2 pages left to read. It will now be a Monday and Julian can finish reading the last 2 pages.

step_outline: Thursday to Sunday $$\rightarrow 6\times 2+11\times 2=34$$ pages  $$348-34=314$$  $1$ week (Monday to Sunday) $$\rightarrow 11\times 2+6\times 5=52$$ pages  $6$ weeks $$\rightarrow 52\times6=312$$ pages  After reading another $312$ pages in $6$ weeks, Julian has another $(314 -- 312) = 2$ pages left to read.
It will now be a Monday and Julian can finish reading the last $2$ pages.

content.problem_plain: Given that April 15 , 2011 was Friday, what day was May 11 , 2011 ? (A) Thursday (B) Saturday (C) Tuesday (D) Wednesday
content.solution_latex: Counting from April $$15$$, $$2011$$, after $30-15=15$ days it will be April $$30$$, $$2011$$. After $11$ days, it will be May $$11$$, $$2011$$. In total, there are $15+11=26$ days. $26\div 7 =3R5$, which means May $$11$$, $$2011$$ was Wednesday.
content.solution_plain: Counting from April 15 , 2011 , after 30-15=15 days it will be April 30 , 2011 . After 11 days, it will be May 11 , 2011 . In total, there are 15+11=26 days. 26 7 =3R5 , which means May 11 , 2011 was Wednesday.

After $11$ days, it will be May $$11$$, $$2011$$.
In total, there are $15+11=26$ days.
$26\div 7 =3R5$, which means May $$11$$, $$2011$$ was Wednesday.

content.problem_latex:
The month of February of a given year has five Fridays. What day of the week is January $$31$$ of that year?
(A) Tuesday
(B) Thursday
(C) Friday
(D) Saturday
content.problem_plain: The month of February of a given year has five Fridays. What day of the week is January 31 of that year? (A) Tuesday (B) Thursday (C) Friday (D) Saturday
content.solution_latex: There are seven days in a week; so, in order to have five Fridays, a month has at least $$4\times7+1=29$$ days. Since February has at most $$29$$ days, the $$1^{\rm st}$$ and $$29^{\rm th}$$ days of this February must both be Fridays, which means that January $$31$$ is a Thursday.
content.solution_plain: There are seven days in a week; so, in order to have five Fridays, a month has at least 4 7+1=29 days. Since February has at most 29 days, the 1^ st and 29^ th days of this February must both be Fridays, which means that January 31 is a Thursday.

step_outline: There are seven days in a week; so, in order to have five Fridays, a month has at least $$4\times7+1=29$$ days.
Since February has at most $$29$$ days, the $$1^{\rm st}$$ and $$29^{\rm th}$$ days of this February must both be Fridays, which means that January $$31$$ is a Thursday.

content.problem_latex:
A total of $$42$$ chickens and rabbits are caged together. If there are $$124$$ legs in total, how many chickens and rabbits are in the cage, respectively?
(A) 20$; $12
(B) 22$; $20
(C) 20$; $24
(D) 19$; $21
content.problem_plain: A total of 42 chickens and rabbits are caged together. If there are 124 legs in total, how many chickens and rabbits are in the cage, respectively? (A) 20 ; 12 (B) 22 ; 20 (C) 20 ; 24 (D) 19 ; 21

content.problem_latex:
William plans to take $$16$$ days to drive to New York City from his home. However, since there is heavy traffic, he drives $$20$$ miles less each day and arrives in New York City $$8$$ days later than his plan. What is the distance between William\textquotesingle s home and New York City?
(A) 480$$ miles
(B) 1080$$ miles
(C) 960$$ miles
(D) 720$$ miles
(E) 1200$$ miles
content.problem_plain: William plans to take 16 days to drive to New York City from his home. However, since there is heavy traffic, he drives 20 miles less each day and arrives in New York City 8 days later than his plan. What is the distance between William s home and New York City? (A) 480 miles (B) 1080 miles (C) 960 miles (D) 720 miles (E) 1200 miles
content.solution_latex: We set up an equation in terms of $d$ as the distance and $x$ as the speed in miles per day originally. We have  $d=16x=24(x-20)$  $16x=24x-480$  $480=8x$  $x=60$  Thus, the distance between William\textquotesingle s home and New York City is $60\times16=960$ miles.
content.solution_plain: We set up an equation in terms of d as the distance and x as the speed in miles per day originally. We have d=16x=24(x-20) 16x=24x-480 480=8x x=60 Thus, the distance between William s home and New York City is 60 16=960 miles.

content.problem_latex:
A group of students go apple-picking. On average, each boy picks $30$ apples and each girl picks $20$ apples. The average number of apples picked by everyone in this groups is $26$. If there are $15$ boys, then how many girls are there in the group?
(A) 15
(B) 10
(C) 9
(D) 20
(E) 23
content.problem_plain: A group of students go apple-picking. On average, each boy picks 30 apples and each girl picks 20 apples. The average number of apples picked by everyone in this groups is 26 . If there are 15 boys, then how many girls are there in the group? (A) 15 (B) 10 (C) 9 (D) 20 (E) 23
content.solution_latex: Every boy picks $$30-26=4$$ more than the average, so boys pick $$4\times 15=60$$ more in total, which is equal to the number that girls picked less than the average in total. Every girl picks $$26-20=6$$ less than the average, so there are $$60\div 6=10$$ girls.
content.solution_plain: Every boy picks 30-26=4 more than the average, so boys pick 4 15=60 more in total, which is equal to the number that girls picked less than the average in total. Every girl picks 26-20=6 less than the average, so there are 60 6=10 girls.

content.problem_latex:
If today is Monday, which day of the week will it be $$22$$ days later? ．
(A) Sunday
(B) Monday
(C) Tuesday
(D) Wednesday
content.problem_plain: If today is Monday, which day of the week will it be 22 days later? ． (A) Sunday (B) Monday (C) Tuesday (D) Wednesday
content.solution_latex: Since there are 7 days in a week, the day will repeat again after 7 days.  Thus, if today is Monday, it will be Monday again a week later.  $$22\div 7=3$$ week R $$1$$.  The day after $$22$$ days is the same as the day after $$1$$ day.  $$1$$ day after Monday is Tuesday.  Therefore, it will be \textbf{Tuesday} $$22$$ days later.
content.solution_plain: Since there are 7 days in a week, the day will repeat again after 7 days. Thus, if today is Monday, it will be Monday again a week later. 22 7=3 week R 1 . The day after 22 days is the same as the day after 1 day. 1 day after Monday is Tuesday. Therefore, it will be Tuesday 22 days later.

step_outline: Since there are 7 days in a week, the day will repeat again after 7 days.
Thus, if today is Monday, it will be Monday again a week later.
The day after $$22$$ days is the same as the day after $$1$$ day.
$$1$$ day after Monday is Tuesday.
Therefore, it will be \textbf{Tuesday} $$22$$ days later.

content.problem_latex:
In a maths test, the average score of $$40$$ students is $$25$$. The lowest score is $$5$$. At most how many student(s) can get $$100$$ points?
(A) 11
(B) 10
(C) 9
(D) 8
(E) 7
content.problem_plain: In a maths test, the average score of 40 students is 25 . The lowest score is 5 . At most how many student(s) can get 100 points? (A) 11 (B) 10 (C) 9 (D) 8 (E) 7
content.solution_latex: Total points: $$40\times 25=1000$$.  To have as many $100$ points as possible, we should consider there are only two groups of students: everyone gets $5$ points in a group and everyone gets $100$ in the other group.  $$10\times 100=1000$$, so there must be less than $10$ people getting $100$ points.  If there are nine $100$s: $$9\times 100+31\times 5=1055\textgreater1000$$, which is impossible.  If there are eight $100$s:$$8\times 100+32\times 5=960\textless{}1000$$.  Thus, the answer is $$8$$.
content.solution_plain: Total points: 40 25=1000 . To have as many 100 points as possible, we should consider there are only two groups of students: everyone gets 5 points in a group and everyone gets 100 in the other group. 10 100=1000 , so there must be less than 10 people getting 100 points. If there are nine 100 s: 9 100+31 5=1055 1000 , which is impossible. If there are eight 100 s: 8 100+32 5=960 1000 . Thus, the answer is 8 .

step_outline: Total points: $$40\times 25=1000$$.
To have as many $100$ points as possible, we should consider there are only two groups of students: everyone gets $5$ points in a group and everyone gets $100$ in the other group.
$$10\times 100=1000$$, so there must be less than $10$ people getting $100$ points.
If there are nine $100$s: $$9\times 100+31\times 5=1055\textgreater1000$$, which is impossible.
If there are eight $100$s:$$8\times 100+32\times 5=960\textless{}1000$$.
Thus, the answer is $$8$$.

content.problem_latex:
Niki usually leaves her cell phone on. If her cell phone is on but she is not actually using it, the battery will last for $$24$$ hours. If she is using it constantly, the battery will last for only $$3$$ hours. Since the last recharge, her phone has been on $$9$$ hours, and during that time she has used it for $$60$$ minutes. If she doesn\textquotesingle t use it any more but leaves the phone on, how many more hours will the battery last?
(A) 7
(B) 8
(C) 11
(D) 14
content.problem_plain: Niki usually leaves her cell phone on. If her cell phone is on but she is not actually using it, the battery will last for 24 hours. If she is using it constantly, the battery will last for only 3 hours. Since the last recharge, her phone has been on 9 hours, and during that time she has used it for 60 minutes. If she doesn t use it any more but leaves the phone on, how many more hours will the battery last? (A) 7 (B) 8 (C) 11 (D) 14
content.solution_latex: When not being used, the cell phone uses up $$\dfrac{1}{24}$$ of its battery per hour. When being used, the cell phone uses up $$\dfrac{1}{3}$$ of its battery per hour. Since Niki\textquotesingle s phone has been on for $$9$$ hours, of those $$8$$ simply on and~~being used to talk, $$8\left(\dfrac{1}{24}\right)+1\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)=\dfrac{2}{3}$$ of its battery has been used up. To drain the remaining $$\dfrac{1}{3}$$ the phone can last for $$\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{3}}{\dfrac{1}{24}}=\boxed{(\text{B})8}$$ more hours without being used.
content.solution_plain: When not being used, the cell phone uses up 1 24 of its battery per hour. When being used, the cell phone uses up 1 3 of its battery per hour. Since Niki s phone has been on for 9 hours, of those 8 simply on and~~being used to talk, 8 ( 1 24 )+1 ( 1 3 )= 2 3 of its battery has been used up. To drain the remaining 1 3 the phone can last for 1 3 1 24 = ( B )8 more hours without being used.

step_outline: When not being used, the cell phone uses up $$\dfrac{1}{24}$$ of its battery per hour.
When being used, the cell phone uses up $$\dfrac{1}{3}$$ of its battery per hour.

Since Niki\textquotesingle s phone has been on for $$9$$ hours, of those $$8$$ simply on and~~being used to talk, $$8\left(\dfrac{1}{24}\right)+1\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)=\dfrac{2}{3}$$ of its battery has been used up.
To drain the remaining $$\dfrac{1}{3}$$ the phone can last for $$\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{3}}{\dfrac{1}{24}}=\boxed{(\text{B})8}$$ more hours without being used.

content.problem_latex:
James makes a long noodle. He takes some bites of the noodle, each time eating $5$ cm of the noodle from the middle of only one piece. At last, he has $12$ pieces of the noodle with the total length of $24$ cm. How long was the entire noodle in cm at the beginning? (Adapted from 2022 AMC 8 Problem, Question \#11)
(A) 94
(B) 89
(C) 80
(D) 79
(E) 75
content.problem_plain: James makes a long noodle. He takes some bites of the noodle, each time eating 5 cm of the noodle from the middle of only one piece. At last, he has 12 pieces of the noodle with the total length of 24 cm. How long was the entire noodle in cm at the beginning? (Adapted from 2022 AMC 8 Problem, Question #11) (A) 94 (B) 89 (C) 80 (D) 79 (E) 75
content.solution_latex: There are $12$ pieces of noodle left, so he takes $12-1=11$ bites of the noodle. Thus, the length of the noodle he ate is $11\times5=55$ cm. The total length of the entire noodle was $55+24=79$ cm.
content.solution_plain: There are 12 pieces of noodle left, so he takes 12-1=11 bites of the noodle. Thus, the length of the noodle he ate is 11 5=55 cm. The total length of the entire noodle was 55+24=79 cm.

step_outline: There are $12$ pieces of noodle left, so he takes $12-1=11$ bites of the noodle.
Thus, the length of the noodle he ate is $11\times5=55$ cm.
The total length of the entire noodle was $55+24=79$ cm.

content.problem_latex:
Barb likes to help her father with housework. She dusts every $3$ days, sweeps every $4$ days, and cooks dinner every $5$ days. If she does all $3$ chores on one Sunday, she next does all $3$ on the same day on a ．
(A) Wednesday
(B) Thursday
(C) Friday
(D) Saturday
content.problem_plain: Barb likes to help her father with housework. She dusts every 3 days, sweeps every 4 days, and cooks dinner every 5 days. If she does all 3 chores on one Sunday, she next does all 3 on the same day on a ． (A) Wednesday (B) Thursday (C) Friday (D) Saturday
content.solution_latex: The least common multiple of $3$, $4$ and $5$ is $60$. We know that $60\div7=8\ldots\ldots4$, then the remainder tells us that it will be on Thursday.
content.solution_plain: The least common multiple of 3 , 4 and 5 is 60 . We know that 60 7=8 4 , then the remainder tells us that it will be on Thursday.

step_outline: The least common multiple of $3$, $4$ and $5$ is $60$.
We know that $60\div7=8\ldots\ldots4$, then the remainder tells us that it will be on Thursday.

content.problem_latex:
Given March $$25$$ of a certain year is Monday, what day of the week would May $$1$$ fall on that year? ．
(A) Tuesday
(B) Wednesday
(C) Thursday
(D) Friday
content.problem_plain: Given March 25 of a certain year is Monday, what day of the week would May 1 fall on that year? ． (A) Tuesday (B) Wednesday (C) Thursday (D) Friday
content.solution_latex: $$1$$ May is $$6+30+1=37$$ days after March $$25$$. Since $$37\div7=5 \text{ R }2$$, $$5$$ weeks after is still Monday and $$2$$ days after that is a Wednesday.

content.problem_latex:
How many two-digit numbers are divisible by $3$ but not by $6$?
(A) 14
(B) 15
(C) 17
(D) 30
(E) None of the above
content.problem_plain: How many two-digit numbers are divisible by 3 but not by 6 ? (A) 14 (B) 15 (C) 17 (D) 30 (E) None of the above
content.solution_latex: There are $99$ $4-3 = 33$-multiples of $3$ less than $100$.  $3$ of them are one-digit numbers: $3$, $6$ and $9$.  Hence there are $30$ $2$-digit multiples of $3$.  Similarly, there are $96 + 6 -1 = 16 -1 = 15$ $2$-digit multiples of $6$.  Hence, the answer is 30 - 15 = \textbf{15.}  Answer： \textbf{(B)}

Hence, the answer is 30 - 15 = \textbf{15.}  Answer： \textbf{(B)}

(A) 210
(B) 40
(C) 30
(D) 20
(E) 10
content.problem_plain: Water from the first faucet fills the swimming pool in 70 minutes. Water from each of the two other faucets fills the same swimming pool 3 times faster. In how many minutes will the swimming pool be filled if all three faucets are opened? (A) 210 (B) 40 (C) 30 (D) 20 (E) 10
content.solution_latex: The efficiency of the first faucet is $\frac1{70}$ and that of the other two is $\frac3{70}$.  Thus, it takes $1\div (\frac1{70}+\frac3{70}\times2)=10$ minutes to fill the pool.

We know that $$\frac{A\left( p+.25 \right)}{100}=\frac{28000p}{100}+\frac{\left( p+2 \right)\left( A-28000 \right)}{100}$$  We can now try to solve for $$A$$:  $$\left( p+.25 \right)A=28000p+Ap+2A-28000p-56000$$  $$0.25A=2A-56000$$  $$A=32000$$  so the answer is $$\boxed{ \text {B}}$$. Notice that　  $$T=p\textbackslash\%\cdot 28000+\left( p+2 \right)\textbackslash\%\cdot \left( A-28000 \right)$$  $$\textasciitilde\textasciitilde\textasciitilde\textasciitilde$$$$=\left[ p\textbackslash\%\cdot 28000+p\textbackslash\%\cdot \left( A-28000 \right) \right]+2\textbackslash\%\cdot \left( A-28000 \right)$$  $$\textasciitilde\textasciitilde\textasciitilde\textasciitilde$$$$=p\textbackslash\%\cdot A+2\textbackslash\%\cdot \left( A-28000 \right)$$  We are also given that  $$T=\left( p+0.25 \right)\textbackslash\%\cdot A=p\textbackslash\%\cdot A+0.25\textbackslash\%\cdot A$$  Thus, $$p\textbackslash\%\cdot A+2\textbackslash\%\cdot \left( A-28000 \right)=p\textbackslash\%\cdot A+0.25\textbackslash\%\cdot A$$  $$2\textbackslash\%\cdot \left( A-28000 \right)=0.25\textbackslash\%\cdot A$$  Solve for $$A$$ to obtain $$A=32000$$. $$\boxed{ \text {B}}$$

content.problem_latex:
Grandma made some cheese dumplings and some blueberry dumplings. Altogether, she made $$31$$ dumplings. If she had made $$11$$ more cheese dumplings, then there would be the same number of blueberry dumplings as cheese dumplings. How many cheese dumplings did grandma make?（2009 Math Kangaroo Problem, Question \#19）
(A) 10
(B) 21
(C) 20
(D) 15
